quotes.verbatim_quote:
IF YOU HEAR Hm TO 1, YOU MAY
THINK lm V7 TO 1 INSTEAD
quotes.why_load_bearing: Reinterprets ii-I as ii-V-I for back-cycling opportunities.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
if you go back 2 chords from any chord, you get the V and the V OF
THE V.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines back-cycling depth in cycle-of-fifths terms.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
sometimes when back cycling, you can
use chords a % step higher than normal, following with chords that are the normal ones for back
cycling
quotes.why_load_bearing: Allows chromatic chord substitution within back-cycle.

quotes.verbatim_quote: You can also use chords a bSth higher than those in the cycle if you like the sound.

Sometimes, if the song is too fast for chords, or for a different sound, you may harmonize the
melody with notes that are a 3rd, 6th, 10th, 13th, or 17th interval BELOW the melody.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Names specific interval-harmonization technique for fast melodies.

quotes.verbatim_quote: There are many useful ideas that guitarists can learn from the study of piano arrangements.
The term triad refers to any chord with 3 notes in it.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines triad term used throughout chapter.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
When the tones of the triad are arranged as closely as possible, the triad is in what is called CLOSE
OR CLOSED VOICING.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines closed voicing — central concept of triad chapter.

quotes.verbatim_quote: There are only 12 different forms of the close voicing major triad (for any key) on the guitar.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the finite catalog of close-voiced triads.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
I to IV is probably the most often used sound in many types of music that
are based on triads, and it should be memorized as soon as possible all over the neck.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Identifies I-IV motion as the priority memorization target.
